_comment: Curated subset of MITRE ATT&CK (Enterprise) techniques for demo/offline RAG retrieval. Not the full ATT&CK dataset — for production use, sync against the official STIX bundle at https://github.com/mitre-attack/attack-stix-data.

techniques.name: Command and Scripting Interpreter
techniques.text: Adversaries may abuse command and script interpreters such as PowerShell, cmd.exe, bash, or Python to execute commands, scripts, or binaries during post-exploitation. PowerShell is frequently used for living-off-the-land execution because it is trusted, pre-installed, and highly capable.

techniques.name: Scheduled Task/Job
techniques.text: Adversaries may abuse task scheduling functionality (schtasks, cron, at) to execute malicious code at a specific time or on a recurring interval, often used to establish persistence after initial access.

techniques.name: Process Injection
techniques.text: Adversaries may inject code into the address space of another process to evade process-based defenses and elevate privileges. Common techniques include DLL injection, process hollowing, and reflective loading.

techniques.name: Obfuscated Files or Information
techniques.text: Adversaries may encode, encrypt, pack, or otherwise obfuscate malicious payloads, scripts, or command-line arguments (e.g. base64-encoded PowerShell) to make static detection and analysis harder.

techniques.name: OS Credential Dumping
techniques.text: Adversaries may dump credentials from the operating system and software, such as LSASS memory or the SAM database, using tools like Mimikatz, secretsdump, or built-in utilities, to obtain account hashes and passwords.

techniques.name: Valid Accounts
techniques.tactic: Defense Evasion, Persistence, Privilege Escalation, Initial Access
techniques.text: Adversaries may use compromised or stolen credentials for legitimate accounts to bypass access controls and blend in with normal user activity, evading detection that targets malware or exploit-based intrusions.

techniques.name: Application Layer Protocol
techniques.text: Adversaries may communicate using application-layer protocols such as HTTP, HTTPS, or DNS to blend command-and-control traffic with legitimate network traffic and evade network-based detection.

techniques.name: Proxy
techniques.text: Adversaries may use a connection proxy, such as Tor exit nodes, VPS relays, or compromised infrastructure, to direct command-and-control traffic and obscure the true source or destination of the communication.

techniques.name: Ingress Tool Transfer
techniques.text: Adversaries may transfer tools or other files from an external system into a compromised environment, often via HTTP downloads, to stage additional malware or post-exploitation tooling.

techniques.name: Exfiltration Over C2 Channel
techniques.text: Adversaries may steal data by exfiltrating it over an existing command-and-control channel rather than a separate exfiltration channel, blending stolen data transfer with normal beaconing traffic.

techniques.name: Data Encrypted for Impact
techniques.text: Adversaries, most commonly associated with ransomware, may encrypt data on target systems to interrupt availability and extort payment for restoring access, often after credential theft and lateral movement.

techniques.name: Phishing
techniques.text: Adversaries may send phishing messages with malicious attachments or links to gain access to victim systems, frequently used as the initial access vector before deploying malware or harvesting credentials.

techniques.name: User Execution
techniques.text: Adversaries may rely on a user opening a malicious file, clicking a link, or enabling macros to execute their payload, often as the follow-through step after a phishing email is delivered.

techniques.name: Network Service Discovery
techniques.text: Adversaries may attempt to get a listing of services running on remote hosts, including those that may be vulnerable to remote exploitation, using tools such as nmap or masscan during reconnaissance.

techniques.name: Remote Services
techniques.text: Adversaries may use valid accounts to log into remote services such as RDP, SSH, or SMB/WinRM to move laterally within a network once initial access has been established.
